title: Our 5 Days of Golf in Ireland: 6 Courses You Need to Play
body:
Six months ago, the three of us flew to Ireland for what turned out to be the trip of our lives. Five days, six golf courses, zero pints of Guinness for our group, and a level of golf that made the rest of the year feel like a slow correction.

We're going to walk you through the whole thing. What we played, where we stayed, what we'd do differently, and whether you should take the trip yourself. The short version is yes, you should. The long version is below.

## The Setup

We booked the trip through Hidden Links, who plan golf trips throughout Ireland and Scotland. If you've never used a trip-planning company before, the value gets obvious about ninety seconds into the experience. We landed at Shannon Airport off a red-eye from Boston, and Mike, our coach driver for the week, was waiting with a Wi-Fi-equipped leather coach big enough to hold twelve people. He didn't leave our side for five days. Tee times were locked, hotels were booked, dinner reservations were handled. Every place we showed up, they already knew our names.

Mike was a story unto himself. Local guy, lived in the area his whole life, and the kind of Irish accent you can't unhear once you've heard it. "Welcome to Killarney." We are still saying that phrase. He'd narrate the drive between courses: when a course was built, what the locals thought of it, what kind of people lived in each town. If you go through Hidden Links, take the coach. It adds something to the trip you can't put a price on.

## Day 1: Old Head Golf Links

![Old Head Golf Links from above](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2025-09-23_at_9.03.38_AM_4_fcxok8.jpg)

We didn't waste any time. We landed Tuesday morning, got in the coach, and drove two and a half hours straight to Old Head. The way to start an Irish golf trip is to take a swing within hours of your plane touching down, before you have a chance to think about how tired you are.

Old Head is the course I was most excited about and most nervous about. It's the one whose drone footage circulates every spring. I was sure we were going to start at the best place we'd see all week and watch the rest of the trip slide downhill from there. That didn't happen. But I was right about Old Head being incredible.

The course sits on a peninsula that juts out into the Atlantic like the land just forgot to stop. There's a lighthouse you can see from most of the round. There are cliffs you're told flat out not to walk past. They have red spray paint and little warning signs at the edges of greens that say if your ball is past this point, leave it, you'll fall and die. And we did walk between holes along the edge of those cliffs. Some of the most spectacular routing I've ever seen.

![Walking path along the Old Head cliffs](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2025-09-23_at_9.11.45_AM_tsiunh.jpg)

Before the round there's a tradition where you shake the head pro's hand through a hole in a stone. Apparently it dates back to settling deals by a handshake through the rock. They incorporate it into your starter ceremony. You feel like you're being inducted into something.

The comparison everyone makes: Old Head is Pebble Beach on steroids. People talk about Pebble like it's the ocean course in America. From what I've seen, Pebble is on the ocean. Old Head is on the ocean for every single hole. After the opening hole, you either see the Atlantic from the tee box, the fairway, the green, or all three. We had perfect weather. A little drizzle to start, then the sun came out. Lucky for our first day.

If you have one course on your bucket list out there, this is probably the one. Just know it's going to set the bar very high.

## The Hotel: Killarney Park

After Old Head, we drove an hour and forty-five minutes north to Killarney and checked into the Killarney Park Hotel. I'm going to make a claim that sounds like an exaggeration and then defend it: this is the nicest hotel I have ever stayed in or expect to ever stay in.

The whole thing was recently renovated. Every room has a sitting area. The bathroom in mine was solid granite, including the shower. The maid service came through what felt like four times a day. I walked in once and the dirty clothes I'd been golfing in all day had been folded into a neat stack next to my clean ones. That is the level of hotel we are talking about.

Breakfast wasn't a buffet. It was a sit-down meal where they bring you a tray of pastries the moment you sit down and then take your order. We ate there both mornings we stayed.

Killarney itself is a great town to come back to after a round. There's a main street with shops, restaurants, and a bar with live music. We had dinner at Treyvaud's the first night, a restaurant Hidden Links booked for us. Walking distance from the hotel, great food, the kind of place where the table next to you is having a louder time than you are and you're rooting for them.

## Day 2: Waterville Golf Links

![Waterville Golf Links from above](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2025-09-23_at_2.38.07_PM_w6kprj.jpg)

Hour and fifteen minutes from Killarney. Waterville has one of the most distinctive logos in golf, a little rabbit, and the best pro shop we saw all week. Bar none. Not just the standard logoed polos and ball markers, but the kind of unique stuff that actually makes a souvenir worth bringing home. I bought a head cover with a bunny on it that's been on my driver ever since.

Waterville plays more like classic links than Old Head does. Old Head has links elements but it's still its own thing. Waterville is the real deal. Tall grass along the fairways, hard fast turf, the wind off the ocean shaping every shot. They had wrapped the flagsticks in rubber bands the day we played because the flags whip so hard in the wind that they'll shred themselves.

We were lucky again with weather. They told us about days where you need a four iron from 140 yards. We had something more reasonable, but enough to make a few caddie consultations interesting.

Speaking of caddies: take one. Every course. No exceptions. We'll come back to this.

## Day 2 (Still): Dooks Golf Links

Hour out from Waterville, we played Dooks in the afternoon. Logo is a little toad. Vibe was the most relaxed of the trip. Caddies riding on the side of golf carts, less buttoned up than anywhere else, a course that feels like a great deal even though "budget course in Ireland" still means a real tee time fee.

![Dooks Golf Links at sunset](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/IMG_6300_cq7f4s.jpg)

This is where you find out that every course on the itinerary is going to have ocean views. We didn't realize how much coastline we'd be playing along until we got there. Dooks starts inland enough that you're not on the water right away. Then you come around a tee box on what I think was the start of the back nine, and the Atlantic is right there behind you. We played the back half at sunset. One of our favorite photos of the entire trip came off that hole.

![The three of us at Dooks](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/IMG_6294_wpyjsd.jpg)

A note about Old Head being so good early in the trip: the worry I had, that everything after would feel like a letdown, was completely wrong. Dooks was awesome. Different than Old Head, but awesome. Every course we played, the answer to "was that worth it?" was yes.

That night we ate at The Haro back in Killarney. The owners of the coach business own the restaurant too, so they came over and sat with us for a while. We learned the Irish habit of standing around chatting for an hour before sitting down to eat. After 36 holes, that takes some adjustment, especially when you're the three Americans sipping Cokes while the table negotiates which Guinness to start with. Nobody made it weird. The food was great.

## Day 3: Tralee Golf Club

![Tralee Golf Club front nine](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2025-09-23_at_9.16.23_AM_2_h7pdzc.jpg)

Forty-five minutes from Killarney, 7 a.m. tee time. We were the first group out. It was foggy enough that the opening tee shot was basically into a wall. You couldn't see where the ball landed. Then as we played, the fog burned off and the ocean materialized off our left like a magic trick.

This was the day we had bad weather. The only really bad weather we got the whole trip. And frankly, if you're going to Ireland and you don't get one day of properly Irish weather, you got cheated. I'm glad we had it. We played through wind and rain and the kind of cold where you put a layer on, take it off ten minutes later, put it back on after three more minutes.

This is also the moment to bring up rain gear. Pay for it. Buy the $200 jacket. Noah and I both grabbed cheaper jackets at the airport on the way out (the PGA store version), and we paid for it. Beau had the real gear and was dry the whole day. The Under Armour rain pants we all bought were great. Those held up. But the jacket is the piece that matters.

Tralee has some of the best par threes I've played anywhere. The first one has castle ruins behind it. There are sheep grazing on the hillside above one of the tee boxes. Behind a couple of others — and I am not making this up — the actual cows that produce Kerrygold butter were grazing in a field. The caddie pointed them out: "See those? That's the Kerrygold."

You don't get that on a Tuesday at your home course.

## The Ferry to Doonbeg

After Tralee we headed northwest to Doonbeg, our base for the last two nights. The drive from Tralee to Doonbeg is about two and a half hours if you take the long way around. Mike knew about the ferry across the Shannon Estuary that saves you close to an hour. We took it, paid maybe twenty bucks, and crossed the water on a ferry watching the Irish coast slide by. We would never have known about it without Hidden Links.

You pull up to Trump International Doonbeg and the property itself looks like a castle. Massive, stone, sitting on the dunes. We didn't play that night. We got in, ate dinner at the Dunes Bar inside the resort, and crashed. (Order around the Trump chicken sandwich. The sauce situation is questionable.)

## Day 4 Morning: Lahinch Golf Club

![Lahinch Golf Club](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Image_9-23-25_at_2.57_PM_2_n0fcmb.jpg)

Forty minutes from Doonbeg to Lahinch. If you're paying attention you'll notice we're packing a lot into each day. That's the trip. The good news is the weeks of soreness pay off later.

This was Noah's favorite course of the trip. Beau goes back and forth between Lahinch and Doonbeg depending on what day you ask. Mine was Doonbeg, but Lahinch was a hair behind.

Here's what makes Lahinch different. Every other course in Ireland we played was straight links, on the ocean, in the dunes, beautiful in the same family of ways. Lahinch had all of that, and it also had a town. The village of Lahinch sits right next to the course, and from several holes you can see the houses, the church, the streets. It's the first time on the trip a course felt rooted in a community instead of stuck out on a peninsula by itself. I loved it.

The course was originally designed by Old Tom Morris and then later redesigned, but they kept some of the original holes. Two of them are blind shots. There's a blind tee shot into a fairway that requires you to wait while someone walks ahead and changes a marker flag. Then there's a fully blind par three. You're staring at a giant grass mound with a white rock on top, and you have to fly your tee shot over it into a green you cannot see. You walk down into a bowl and there it is. Hit it short into the mound and you're toast.

![The Hobbit-style halfway house at Lahinch](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/View_recent_photos_9_glfmxl.png)

The halfway house at Lahinch deserves its own paragraph. Aesthetically it is the best snack stop on the planet. It looks like a Hobbit hole built into the dunes. Crows circling, the wind whipping, a little window where someone hands you a snack and a flavored sports drink that isn't quite Gatorade. Whether the food itself is great is less important than the fact that you are eating it in this place.

## Day 4 Evening: Trump International Doonbeg

Drove back to Doonbeg and closed out the trip there. This was my favorite course of the week. If you'd asked me on day three what my number one would be, I'd have said something different. By the time we walked off the 18th here, the answer was Doonbeg.

The opening hole might be the coolest first tee shot I've ever played. You tee off right in front of the clubhouse with everyone on the patio watching, the fairway runs straight, and the first green is tucked back into a massive dune that looks like it was carved out by hand.

But the real moment is the 18th. Doonbeg's closing hole was the best finishing hole of our trip. I'll fight you on that. The castle clubhouse sits in the background to your left. The Atlantic is on your right. We finished the round at sunset. If you have ever wondered what heaven looks like, it looks like the 18th tee at Doonbeg at 7 p.m. in September.

![The 18th tee box at Doonbeg](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2025-09-23_at_2.49.35_PM_2_g6bkdf.jpg)

![The 18th fairway at Doonbeg at sunset](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2025-09-23_at_9.27.49_AM_wz4ios.jpg)

That was day four. Day five was the drive back to Shannon Airport and the flight home.

## Things We'd Tell You to Do

A few things that mattered, in no particular order:

- **Take a caddie at every course.** Not optional. You're going to play blind shots, face wind you can't read, think a 150-yard shot is a nine iron when it's actually an eight. You need someone who knows the land to tell you the answer. They're also great company. By the second hole they know your game well enough to call your clubs for you.
- **Pay for real rain gear.** Don't be us at Tralee.
- **Bring layers. Then bring more layers.** You'll wear all of them in a single round, then have to wear them again the next day.
- **Use Hidden Links.** Quick disclosure: they sponsored the trip. We're recommending them anyway because every part of the experience earned it. Mike the coach driver, the locked-in tee times, the dinner reservations, the way the whole week ran on rails. We'd book through them again on our own dime without thinking twice. The coach alone is worth it.
- **If you don't drink, don't worry about it.** We didn't, and nobody at any course, pub, or restaurant treated us like we were missing something. The hospitality is the hospitality regardless.
- **Get the breakfast at the Killarney Park.**

## So, Should You Go?

Yes. This is the part of the post where I'm supposed to write a balanced "is it right for you" section, and I'm not going to do that. The answer is yes. If you care about golf at all, you have to play in Ireland once. At thirty, forty, fifty, whatever. Find the year, find the budget, get a group together, and go.

Every course we played was on the ocean. Every halfway house was a story. Every caddie was a character. The weather was a character. The hotel was a character. Mike was a character. There is no version of this trip that doesn't change how you feel about the game.

American golf is great. We play it every week. But after a week in Ireland, you understand what people mean when they say "golf the way it was meant to be played." The phrase stops sounding like a slogan. You feel it walking off the second tee. Cliffs and wind and grass and ocean, and a guy in his sixties walking your bag and reading the wind off the heather.

We're already talking about going back. There are courses we didn't play that we have to play. Royal County Down, Royal Portrush, Ballybunion. Lahinch and Doonbeg again. Old Head in heavy weather to see what it really feels like.

If you're thinking about it, stop thinking about it. Book it. We're happy to answer any question you have. And if you want help putting an itinerary together, talk to Hidden Links. They earned the recommendation.

That's the trip. Until the next one.

excerpt: Five days, six courses, one trip that makes American golf feel like a warmup. A course-by-course guide to Old Head, Waterville, Dooks, Tralee, Lahinch & Doonbeg.
author: Cameron
